element_introduction_order.mastery_context: First WWF console game to feature cage matches; requires spatial awareness and cage wall mechanics

element_introduction_order.first_appears: WWF Championship / Tag Team Championship modes

rhythm_pattern.pacing_descriptor: Match-based cyclical rhythm with sustained tension punctuated by brief offensive windows; escalates through championship progression

difficulty_curve.early_game: Introduction to basic one-on-one matches establishes core mechanics without overwhelming opponent variety
difficulty_curve.mid_game: Tag team matches and cage matches introduce spatial complexity and coordinate management
difficulty_curve.late_game: Championship modes escalate difficulty through sequential opponent matchups with varied wrestler movesets (all wrestlers share identical movesets mechanically, but roster variety provides psychological variety)
notable_spikes: Transition to steel cage match (first cage match variant in WWF console games; introduces wall-based spatial constraints)
Championship mode progression (cumulative fatigue across multiple consecutive matches)
Tag team championship mode (requires managing two wrestlers and coordinating partner switches)

notable_level_patterns.description: Basic wrestling match with regular match rules; teaches fundamental combat loop of striking, grappling, and running attacks
Enclosed cage environment changes spatial strategy; walls provide both obstacle and environmental hazard; first WWF console game to feature cage matches after arcade appearances
Two-versus-two format requiring partner coordination; introduces tag timing and fatigue management across multiple wrestlers
Sequential progression format where player defeats all roster members; escalates through roster variety and cumulative fatigue
notable_level_patterns.example_level: WWF Championship mode, Tag Team Championship mode
